summary: McKenzie posts a final call for beta testers for Bingo Card Creator 2.5, listing all reported bugs and his responses to each. He categorizes issues into fixed, by-design, and deferred, showing pragmatic triage of a solo developer shipping cross-platform software. The post reveals the friction of building a Java desktop app that must work on both Windows and Mac, with platform-specific UI expectations creating constant tension. He closes with a technical tip for Ant users about zip file permissions on Mac.
key_points: Cross-platform desktop software requires constant compromise between platform conventions, especially Mac vs Windows UI expectations
McKenzie triages bugs pragmatically: fixing what impacts customers, deferring cosmetic issues, and defending intentional design choices
He takes responsibility for unclear UI rather than blaming users, noting that if testers missed button descriptions it's his fault
Backwards compatibility with existing paying customers constrains what a solo developer can change, even when the change would be objectively better
money_quotes: if you guys didn't see it that is my fault.
I'm sort of loathe to remove functionality, particularly functionality that a few hundred paying customers may be using already.
You may be interested in knowing that applications can write to Program Files in Vista. Really, try it — MS hacked around their own security system to avoid breaking legacy apps.
Much love to the testers that discovered this before my users did.
key_insight: Effective bug triage for a solo software business means fixing what impacts paying customers, owning UX failures, and deferring edge cases without guilt.
